summary: Using Android marketshare data from 2010-2017, this analysis demonstrates that Android devices are becoming increasingly outdated over time, with nearly half of the two billion active devices being at least two years behind on updates as of 2017. The data shows that new Android version adoption is slowing down, creating over one billion devices stuck on old versions that will likely never be updated. This contrasts sharply with iOS, where new versions achieve 50% adoption within months, and compares unfavorably even to Windows XP, which received twelve years of security updates.
key_points: Nearly 50% of Android's 2+ billion active devices are two years out of date, with approximately zero chance of ever updating
Android version adoption is slowing over time - recent releases show shallower uptake curves than previous versions
The data understates the problem by measuring API versions (not point releases), showing only Play Store users, and using marketshare rather than absolute device counts
iOS 11 achieved ~50% marketshare within two months vs <1% for the latest Android version released three months prior
Even Windows XP received 12+ years of updates, while typical Android devices from the same era got 1.5-2 years
money_quotes: At this point, we should expect that there are more than one billion devices that are two years out of date! Given Android's update model, we should expect approximately 0% of those devices to ever get updated to a modern version of Android.
This is tremendously unfair to Windows XP, which was released on 10/2001 and got security updates until 4/2014, twelve and a half years later.

iOS 11 was released two months ago and it now has just under 50% iOS marketshare despite November's numbers coming before the release of the iPhone X (this is compared to < 1% marketshare for the latest Android version, which was released in August).

It looks like Android is still likely to stay behind iOS in terms of updates for a while. Google has promised that its latest phone (Pixel 2, 10/2017) will get updates for three years... But if you have an iPhone, the latest version of iOS (released 9/2017) works on devices back to the iPhone 5S (released 9/2013).
Android's scale is unprecedented in the desktop world. The were roughly 200 million PCs sold in 2017. Samsung alone has been selling that many mobile devices per year since 2008.
key_insight: Data-driven analysis reveals that Android's update problem is worse than commonly understood and getting progressively worse over time, with the unprecedented scale of Android deployment creating billions of permanently outdated, potentially insecure devices that will persist for decades.
